title: 08-1-1 Duke duPlessis - Utilizing Low Grade Coal and Oil Sands Residues for Producing Power
text: Utilizing Low Grade Coal and Oil Sands Residues for Producing Power, Hydrogen and Chemicals in Western Canada-Opportunities and Challenges IAGT- NRC Gas Turbine Conference October 20-21, 2008 Ottawa Duke du Plessis Senior Advisor and Research Manager Alberta Energy Research Institute Alberta Finance and Enterprise Calgary, Alberta Canada Presentation Outline z Alberta Energy Research Institute- Overview z Western Canadian Energy Resources z Oil sands challenges and opportunities z The CO2 issue z Gasification- Key technology platform for oil sands, coal and clean power generation z IGCC with CO2 capture z RD&D needs z Conclusions Mission: Enhance the development of clean energy resources through research, technology and innovation. Mandate: z Position Alberta for the future in energy z Add value to Alberta’s energy resources Strategy: z Invest in technology development projects with industry z Partnerships & International collaboration z Focus on Technology Platforms: e.g. Gasification, Upgrading and Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) Alberta Energy Research Institute (AERI) (Energy Technology Arm of the Alberta Government) www.aeri.ab.ca Fossil Reserves Concentrated in Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin Oil 109 bbls Gas 1012 cu.ft. Bitumen 109 bbls Coal 109 tonnes Remaining Reserves 1.5 39 173 34 Annual Production 0.19 4.8 0.48 0.037 Reserves/Production, years 9.4 8.1 360 919 • Proven oil reserves: 2nd after Saudi Arabia • Crude oil producer: 7th in the world • Energy producer: 5th in the world • Natural gas producer:3rd in the world • Largest exporter to USA The Nature of the Oil Sands Resource The Bitumen Value Chain- Product and Market

title: 08-1-1 Duke duPlessis - Utilizing Low Grade Coal and Oil Sands Residues for Producing Power
text: large point-sources Good News: Emitters are close to Oil Fields and Deep Saline Acquifers Integrated CO2 Network( ICO2N) Alliance of 15 of Canada’s large industrial companies, representing •over 100Mt CO2 of annual emissions • 60% of Alberta’s power generation, • 95% of the oil sands producers • Connect large CO2 emitters to EOR and sequestration sites “WCSB has vast potential for CCS in deep saline acquifers and oil fields suitable for enhanced oil recovery” Summary Report by the ICO2N Group of Companies - December 2007. Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin Alberta’s Approach to Reduction of GHG’s z Long term issue – Need to start with practical, achievable objectives z Technology will be a big part of the long-term solution z Introduced multi-sector regulatory-based demand for carbon reductions – Climate Change and Emissions Management Act – Ecotrust Fund z Announced $2 billion fund for CCS projects capturing 5 million tonnes per year of CO2 – Announced June 2008 – Strong response to request for expressions of interest Gasification Drivers, Opportunities & Challenges z Capital cost z Reliability z CO2 & water management z Performance with low quality fuels z Large coal resources z Electricity demand growth z Aging coal plants z Oil sands developments z Natural gas replacement z GHG regulations zProximity of EOR and sequestration sites z Gasification of coal & oil sands residues offers sustainable solutions, BUT face significant challenges Challenges Drivers and Opportunities Upgrading Refining Oxygen Gasification: A Key Technology Platform Slag Sour Shift Acid Gas Removal Combined Cycle

title: 08-1-1 Duke duPlessis - Utilizing Low Grade Coal and Oil Sands Residues for Producing Power
text: cleanup - H2 rich fuel - GE 7FB Frame Turbine - 90 % CO2 Capture ¾Amine scrubbing ¾Oxyfuel combustion ¾ Other emission levels equivalent to NGCC plants Performance & Cost estimates Impact of CO2 Capture on IGCC Performance and Cost Lignite Subbituminous Coal Non- Capture Capture Change % Non- Capture Capture Change % Net Plant Output, MW 571 483 - 25.4 524 481 - 8.2 Plant Efficiency, % (HHV) 40.9 31.5 - 9.4 39.6 32.6 - 8.0 Capital Cost, $/kW Net 5480 7186 + 31 4521 5547 + 27 CO2 emission intensity (t/MWh) 0.86 0.18 - 79 0.87 0.27 - 70 • IGCC performance and costs depends on fuel quality • CO2 capture reduces plant performance and increases plant costs • CO2 emission intensity reduced 70+% Conclusions IGCC with CO2 Capture z Technology enhancements needed to make IGCC more competitive especially for low rank western coals. z IGCC with CO2 capture needs to be demonstrated at full scale to prove the designs and optimize performance z Site specific FEED studies are needed to improve reliability of cost estimates. Existing Site at Genesee Conceptual Siting of 275 MW Plant Courtesy EPCOR EPCOR FEED Study- IGCC with CO2 Capture Siemens Gasifier RD&D to Improve IGCC Performance z Improve feedstock quality. Reduce ash and moisture z High pressure dry solids feed pumps z Advanced gasifiers z Warm gas clean-up z Ion Transport Membranes for oxygen generation z Hydrogen transport membranes z Advanced gas turbines z Integrated SOFC-Gas Turbine system z Enhanced/new CO2 capture

title: 08-1-1 Duke duPlessis - Utilizing Low Grade Coal and Oil Sands Residues for Producing Power
text: Capture WGCU ITM+SOFC Topping Cycle CO2 Capture SF-Slurry Feed 1-S Single Stage DF- Dry Feed F-GT: F-Frame Gas Turbine FB-GT: FB Frame Gas Turbine WGCU- Warm Gas Cleanup ITM- Oxygen Transport Membrane SOFC- Solid Oxide Fuel Cell Adapted from: The Potential Impact of Developing Technologies on the Economics and Performance of Future IGCC Plants, John Plunkett, David Gray, Charles White, Noblis, USA International Pittsburgh Coal Conference, Sept 2008 Reference Case No Capture IGCC with ITM, Warm Gas Cleanup, SOFC topping cycle and CO2 Capture Oxygen to Gasifier and GT ST Hydrogen Air N2/O2 HRSG A Stack Oxygen CO2 to Sequestration Hg Rem SCOHS COS Hyd SCRUB WHB Gasifier Hydrogen to A Coal Oxygen Cathode Anode SOFC HRSG CO2 Dehydration Compression 10 CO2 Recycle O2 10 ITM Efficiency = 60% Adapted from: The Potential Impact of Developing Technologies on the Economics and Performance of Future IGCC Plants, John Plunkett, David Gray, Charles White, Noblis, USA International Pittsburgh Coal Conference, Sept 2008 Warm Gas Cleanup SCOHS-Selective Catalytic Oxidation of H2S at about 150 deg C H2S + 1/2O2 = S + H2O Eliminates the need for sulphur plant Sulphur AERI-Industry Program to Develop Advanced ‘Clean’ Upgrading Technologies z Goal: Develop next generation technologies for upgrading bitumen to high value products more economically and with lower environmental footprint z Screened 100 technologies: selected eight for further development in partnership with process developers and first adopters – Includes 3 gasification technologies Petrochemicals 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 Bitumen SCO Kerosene Diesel Gasoline

title: 08-1-1 Duke duPlessis - Utilizing Low Grade Coal and Oil Sands Residues for Producing Power
text: technologies for upgrading bitumen to high value products more economically and with lower environmental footprint z Screened 100 technologies: selected eight for further development in partnership with process developers and first adopters – Includes 3 gasification technologies Petrochemicals 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 Bitumen SCO Kerosene Diesel Gasoline Benzene C3= C2= $/$ of Bitumen Refined Products SCO Value Captured CO2 Integrated Complex Pratt and Whitney Rocketdyne Technologies Gasifier Pilot Facility at GTI 8/15/08 ●Rapid Mix Burner ●Cooled Liner ●Spray Quench High Pressure Solids Feed Pump (Conceptual) High Mass Flux Entrained Bed Gasifier Conclusions z Driver for gasification and IGCC in Western Canada is conversion of low coal and oil sands residues into clean power, hydrogen and high value products while capturing CO2 z RD&D to improve overall performance and cost competitiveness include – Advanced gasifiers and gas turbines – Advanced gas cleanup technologies – Membranes for producing oxygen and separating hydrogen & CO2 – Advanced CO2 capture technologies – Integration and optimization of all system components. z First of a kind demonstration plants are needed to accelerate commercial acceptance and deployment. The Oil Sands Model “Learning by Doing” Source: Syncrude Ltd. Technology advances over 25+ years have reduced mined oil sands production costs from > US$30 to < $10/barrel
